short_bio: Lena Petrović is a Croatian historian and writer whose work explores the material culture of the eastern Adriatic. Her books have been translated into nine languages.
long_bio_early: Lena Petrović was born in 1978 in Šibenik, a port city on the central Dalmatian coast. She grew up in a family of stonemasons and boatbuilders and spent childhood summers on the island of Zlarin, where her grandfather harvested red coral using methods unchanged since the eighteenth century. These early experiences shaped her lifelong fascination with the relationship between landscape, craft, and commerce.
long_bio_career: She studied history at the University of Zagreb and completed her doctorate at the Central European University in Budapest with a dissertation on Venetian salt monopolies in the fifteenth-century Adriatic. Her first book, Ston and Its Walls (2009), won the Josip Juraj Strossmayer Prize for Historical Writing. She has since published four further works of narrative history, including The Bora Notebooks (2015), which was shortlisted for the European Book Prize, and Adriatic Freight (2021), praised by the Financial Times as the year's finest work of Mediterranean microhistory.
long_bio_current: Petrović is a senior research fellow at the Institute for Historical Sciences in Dubrovnik and a visiting lecturer at Ca' Foscari University in Venice. She divides her time between Dubrovnik and a stone cottage on the island of Korčula, where she is at work on a book about the oyster beds of Mali Ston Bay.
